title: Functional Programming
summary: Write cleaner code using lambda, map, filter, reduce, and list comprehensions

sections.title: Functions as first-class citizens
sections.content: In Python, functions are values — you can pass them around like numbers or strings. This is the heart of functional programming: treating functions as building blocks that can be assigned to variables, passed as arguments, or returned from other functions. Think of it like a factory assembly line where each station (function) performs one transformation and passes the result to the next station. This style often leads to more declarative code — you describe WHAT you want done, not HOW to do it step by step. Python blends procedural, OOP, and functional styles, so you pick the best tool for each job.

sections.title: Lambda, map, and filter in action
sections.content: Process a list of employee salaries — filter, transform, and sum in a functional style
sections.code:
# List of annual salaries for employees
salaries = [45000, 62000, 38000, 75000, 52000, 41000]

# Filter: keep only salaries above $50000 using a lambda (anonymous function)
# lambda x: ... is a one-line function without a def statement
high_earners = list(filter(lambda s: s > 50000, salaries))
print("High earners:", high_earners)

# Map: apply a 10% bonus to every salary
# Each element gets transformed through the lambda function
with_bonus = list(map(lambda s: s * 1.10, high_earners))
print("With bonus:", with_bonus)

# List comprehension — the Pythonic way (more readable than map/filter)
# [expression for item in iterable if condition]
bonus_list = [s * 1.10 for s in salaries if s > 50000]
print("Comprehension:", bonus_list)

# Expected output:
# High earners: [62000, 75000, 52000]
# With bonus: [68200.0, 82500.0, 57200.0]
# Comprehension: [68200.0, 82500.0, 57200.0]

sections.title: List comprehensions vs map/filter — the trade-off
sections.content: List comprehensions are Python's preferred functional-style tool. They combine map and filter into one readable expression: [f(x) for x in iterable if condition]. They're generally faster than map/filter with lambdas because they avoid function call overhead per element. However, if you already have a named function, map(function, iterable) can be cleaner. For simple transformations, use comprehensions. For extremely large datasets (millions of items), consider generator expressions (replace [] with ()) which produce items lazily without building the entire list in memory. For complex multi-step pipelines, good old for loops with explicit variable names may be the clearest option.

sections.title: Don't overuse lambdas — name your functions
sections.content: Lambdas are convenient but reduce readability when complex. Wrong: result = map(lambda x: x * 1.10 if x > 0 else 0, data) — the lambda is doing too much. Correct: define a named function: def apply_bonus(salary): return salary * 1.10 if salary > 0 else 0; result = map(apply_bonus, data). Named functions are easier to test, debug, and reuse.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: You have a list of product prices: [29.99, 49.99, 10.50, 89.99, 5.00, 120.00]. Use a list comprehension to: (1) keep only prices over $20 (the profitable items), and (2) apply a 15% discount to each kept price. Print the resulting list. Then do the same with map() and filter() to compare the syntax.
hints: Which part of the comprehension is the condition to filter items?
Which part is the transformation expression applied to each item?
